name: survey-ai-detection
description:
Detect AI-generated survey responses using multiple heuristic and statistical methods.

Based on academic research (Wu et al. 2025, Yang et al. 2024):
- Linguistic analysis: perplexity scoring, burstiness, vocabulary diversity
- Response pattern analysis: timing, straightlining, semantic similarity between responses
- Statistical methods: response length distribution, lexical richness (TTR), coherence scores
- Behavioral signals: completion time vs expected, copy-paste indicators, IP/device clustering
plan_prompt:
You are a senior survey methodologist and data quality specialist with expertise in AI-generated text detection. Design a comprehensive AI response detection protocol for survey data.

Context: {context}

## Theoretical Foundation

Your plan must be grounded in academic research on AI text detection, survey data quality, and statistical anomaly detection:

**AI Text Detection Research**: The rapidly evolving field of AI-generated text detection provides the methodological foundation. Key references:
- **Mitchell et al. (2023) 'DetectGPT: Zero-Shot Machine-Generated Text Detection Using Probability Curvature'** demonstrates that AI-generated text occupies areas of high log-probability under the generating model, and perturbations of AI text tend to decrease in probability more than perturbations of human text.
- **Gehrmann, Strobelt & Rush (2019) 'GLTR: Statistical Detection and Visualization of Generated Text'** introduces the concept of using the rank distribution of tokens as a detection signal. AI text disproportionately uses high-probability (top-k) tokens, while human text exhibits more lexical diversity and unexpected word choices.
- **Wu et al. (2025)** and **Yang et al. (2024)** provide survey-specific AI detection methods, extending general detection to the structured response context where AI responses tend to exhibit specific patterns: uniform length, high coherence across unrelated questions, absence of personal anecdotes, and formulaic structure.
- **Jawahar, Abdul-Mageed & Lakshmanan (2020)** demonstrate that AI text can be detected through linguistic features: perplexity (how predictable the text is), burstiness (variation in sentence structure and length), and stylometric consistency.

**Linguistic Detection Signals**:
- **Perplexity**: measures how 'surprised' a language model is by the text. Human text typically has higher perplexity (more surprising, less predictable) than AI-generated text. Low perplexity in survey responses suggests AI generation.
- **Burstiness**: measures variation in sentence length and complexity. Human writing exhibits high burstiness (alternating between short and long sentences, simple and complex structures). AI text tends toward uniform sentence length and complexity. Introduced by Tian & Cui (2023).
- **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)**: measures vocabulary diversity. TTR = unique words / total words. AI text often has lower TTR (more repetitive vocabulary) than human text, especially in open-ended survey responses.
- **Hapax Legomena Ratio**: proportion of words that appear only once. Higher in human text.
- **Formality consistency**: human responses vary in formality based on context and emotion. AI responses maintain consistent formality throughout.

**Survey Data Quality Methods** (Groves et al., 2009 'Survey Methodology'):
- **Satisficing theory** (Krosnick, 1991): respondents may take shortcuts (straightlining, random responding) rather than optimizing their answers. AI respondents exhibit a different pattern: over-optimization (too comprehensive, too balanced, too coherent).
- **Speeder detection**: responses completed in less than 1/3 of the median completion time are typically flagged as low quality (Malhotra, 2008).
- **Straightlining detection**: selecting the same response option across all items in a battery (e.g., all 4s on a Likert scale).
- **Attention check questions**: embedded questions with known correct answers to verify engagement.

**Statistical Anomaly Detection**:
- **Mahalanobis distance**: measures how far each response is from the center of the multivariate distribution. Responses with high Mahalanobis distance are statistical outliers.
- **Isolation Forest**: unsupervised anomaly detection algorithm that isolates observations by randomly selecting features and splitting values. Anomalies are easier to isolate (fewer splits required).
- **Semantic similarity clustering**: using text embeddings (e.g., sentence-BERT), calculate pairwise semantic similarity. AI responses to the same question tend to cluster more tightly than human responses.

## Step-by-Step Methodology

Produce a detailed Markdown plan covering each of the following phases:

### Phase 1: Detection Method Selection and Configuration
- Define the multi-layer detection approach. Each layer provides an independent signal; the combination increases accuracy:
  - **Layer 1 - Behavioral Signals** (fastest, no NLP required):
    - Response completion time analysis
    - Straightlining detection
    - Response pattern uniformity (all responses exactly the same length)
    - IP address and device fingerprint clustering
    - Timestamp analysis (exact-second submissions, inhuman speed between questions)
  - **Layer 2 - Linguistic Features** (moderate complexity):
    - Vocabulary diversity (TTR, Hapax ratio)
    - Average sentence length and variation (burstiness)
    - Formality score and consistency
    - Use of hedging language ('I think', 'maybe', 'personally') -- present in human text, often absent in AI text
    - Personal anecdote detection (first-person specific experiences vs. generic responses)
    - Response length distribution analysis
  - **Layer 3 - Statistical Pattern Analysis** (requires full dataset):
    - Semantic similarity clustering (responses that are too similar to each other across different respondents)
    - Cross-question coherence (AI responses to unrelated questions often maintain unnaturally high coherence)
    - Response length uniformity across questions (AI tends toward consistent length; humans vary by question engagement)
    - Distribution analysis: AI responses often cluster differently from human responses on multi-item scales
  - **Layer 4 - Content Quality Analysis** (most nuanced):
    - Specificity vs. generality: human responses include specific details; AI responses tend toward general, comprehensive coverage
    - Emotional authenticity: human responses express genuine frustration, excitement, confusion; AI responses are measured and balanced
    - Factual accuracy of self-reports: AI may confuse or fabricate details about personal experience
    - Structural patterns: AI text often uses lists, parallelism, and balanced 'on the other hand' constructions

### Phase 2: Threshold Calibration
- Define detection thresholds for each signal:
  - Thresholds must balance sensitivity (catching AI responses) against specificity (not falsely flagging humans).
  - Start with conservative thresholds (high specificity) to minimize false positives.
  - Define three flag levels:
    - **High confidence AI**: multiple strong signals triggered. Recommend removal.
    - **Moderate suspicion**: some signals triggered but not conclusive. Recommend manual review.
    - **Low suspicion**: one weak signal. Recommend keeping with note.
- Calibration approach:
  - If a labeled dataset (known human/AI responses) is available: use ROC analysis to select thresholds that maximize the AUC.
  - If no labeled dataset: use statistical outlier detection (flag responses beyond 2 standard deviations on multiple measures).
- Define the false positive tolerance: for survey research, a 5% false positive rate is typically acceptable (better to keep some AI responses than to throw out legitimate human responses).

### Phase 3: Attention Check and Trap Question Design
- Design attention check questions that are difficult for AI to detect and easy for attentive humans:
  - **Instructed response items (IRI)**: 'Please select Strongly Disagree for this question.' Tests whether the respondent is reading carefully.
  - **Consistency checks**: ask the same question twice (rephrased) and check for contradictions. Humans may slightly vary; AI may be perfectly consistent.
  - **Red herring questions**: include a question with a clearly correct factual answer that is tangential to the survey topic. AI may answer correctly but the question seems too easy for a human respondent who might wonder why it is being asked.
  - **Open-ended specificity probes**: 'Describe a specific time when...' AI tends to produce generic examples; humans provide specific personal narratives.
  - **Temporal and contextual probes**: ask about specific recent events, local context, or personal circumstances that require genuine experience.
- Placement strategy: distribute attention checks throughout the survey, not just at the beginning or end.

### Phase 4: Analysis Pipeline Design
- Define the sequential analysis pipeline:
  1. Behavioral signal analysis (completion time, straightlining, IP/device)
  2. Linguistic feature extraction (TTR, burstiness, perplexity, formality)
  3. Statistical pattern analysis (semantic similarity, distribution analysis)
  4. Content quality analysis (specificity, emotional authenticity)
  5. Composite scoring (weighted combination of all signals)
  6. Flag classification (high/moderate/low)
  7. Manual review of flagged responses
  8. Final disposition (remove/review/keep)
- Define the composite scoring formula:
  - Score = w1 * behavioral_score + w2 * linguistic_score + w3 * statistical_score + w4 * content_score
  - Weights should reflect the reliability of each layer (behavioral signals are less reliable alone; linguistic + content together are most reliable)
  - Suggested weights: behavioral = 0.15, linguistic = 0.30, statistical = 0.25, content = 0.30

### Phase 5: Reporting and Documentation
- Define the output format:
  - Per-response report: each response gets a composite score, individual signal scores, flag level, and recommendation.
  - Aggregate report: total responses, flag rate, flag distribution, clean dataset size, data quality assessment.
  - Methodology notes: which methods were applied, thresholds used, false positive rate assessment.
- Define the clean dataset: responses after removing high-confidence AI flags and incorporating manual review decisions.

### Phase 6: Quality Criteria
- Detection must use at least 3 independent signal layers.
- False positive rate must be estimated and documented.
- Manual review must be conducted for all moderate-suspicion flags.
- The clean dataset size must be reported alongside the original dataset size.
- Methodology notes must be transparent enough for peer review.

Format the entire plan as structured Markdown with clear headers, actionable steps, and explicit quality gates.
execute_prompt:
You are a senior survey methodologist and data quality specialist with expertise in AI-generated text detection. Analyze the provided survey responses for AI-generated content.

Context: {context}

Survey response data:
{content}

## Detection Methodology

Follow this rigorous multi-phase detection process based on Mitchell et al. (2023) DetectGPT, Gehrmann et al. (2019) GLTR, Wu et al. (2025), Yang et al. (2024), Tian & Cui (2023), and established survey methodology (Groves et al., 2009; Krosnick, 1991).

### Phase 1: Behavioral Signal Analysis
- Analyze response timing data (if available):
  - Calculate median completion time across all responses.
  - Flag responses completed in less than 1/3 of the median as 'speeders' (Malhotra, 2008).
  - Flag responses completed in less than 1/6 of the median as 'extreme speeders' (almost certainly not genuine human engagement).
  - Analyze inter-question timing: AI-generated responses may show unnaturally uniform time between questions, or near-instantaneous timestamps if pasted in.
- Detect straightlining patterns:
  - For each response, check Likert-scale batteries: did the respondent select the same option for all items?
  - Calculate the standard deviation of responses within each battery. SD = 0 indicates perfect straightlining.
  - Note: straightlining and AI are different issues, but both indicate low-quality data.
- Detect IP/device clustering (if metadata available):
  - Multiple responses from the same IP address or device fingerprint.
  - Multiple responses from unusual geographic locations (e.g., known VPN endpoints).
- Calculate behavioral flag score (0-1): weighted combination of timing, straightlining, and clustering signals.

### Phase 2: Linguistic Feature Extraction
- For each open-ended response, calculate the following linguistic features:
  - **Type-Token Ratio (TTR)**: unique_words / total_words. Human responses in surveys typically show TTR 0.5-0.8 for short responses. AI responses may show lower TTR (more repetitive vocabulary) or artificially high TTR (deliberately varied).
  - **Average Sentence Length**: count words per sentence. AI text tends toward moderate, consistent sentence length (15-25 words). Human text shows more variation.
  - **Sentence Length Standard Deviation (Burstiness)**: measure variation in sentence length. Low SD = low burstiness = more AI-like. Human text typically has SD > 5 words.
  - **Hapax Legomena Ratio**: words appearing only once / total words. Higher in human text (humans use more 'one-off' words).
  - **Formality Score**: measure the use of formal vs. informal language markers. Calculate using the ratio of formal features (passive voice, nominalizations, technical terms) to informal features (contractions, colloquialisms, first person, exclamations). AI text tends toward higher and more consistent formality.
  - **Hedging Language Frequency**: count hedging expressions ('I think', 'maybe', 'sort of', 'kind of', 'probably', 'in my experience', 'personally'). Human responses use more hedging. AI text tends to be more assertive.
  - **First-Person Specific References**: count specific personal references ('my wife', 'last Tuesday', 'at my office', 'when I was in college'). Humans include these naturally; AI tends toward generic examples or carefully constructed generic narratives.
  - **Discourse Markers**: count natural conversation markers ('well', 'you know', 'like', 'actually', 'honestly'). Present in human text, often absent or artificially inserted in AI text.
- Calculate a composite linguistic score (0-1) for each response.

### Phase 3: Response Pattern Analysis (Dataset-Level)
- Analyze patterns across all responses (these signals require the full dataset):
  - **Semantic Similarity Clustering**:
    - For each open-ended question, compute pairwise semantic similarity between all responses using cosine similarity on text embeddings.
    - Calculate the mean pairwise similarity. AI responses to the same question tend to produce higher mean similarity because they draw from similar probability distributions.
    - Identify clusters of highly similar responses (similarity > 0.85). Multiple respondents giving near-identical responses is a strong AI signal.
    - Flag individual responses that are in a tight cluster with 3+ other responses.
  - **Response Length Uniformity Analysis**:
    - For each respondent, calculate the coefficient of variation (CV) of response lengths across all open-ended questions.
    - Low CV (< 0.2) suggests uniform length -- suspicious because humans naturally write more on topics they care about and less on others.
    - AI respondents often produce consistently medium-length responses (100-200 words) regardless of question topic.
  - **Cross-Question Coherence**:
    - Calculate the semantic similarity between a respondent's answers to unrelated questions.
    - High coherence across unrelated questions is suspicious: genuine respondents shift topic, tone, and vocabulary between unrelated questions.
  - **Scale Response Distribution**:
    - For Likert-scale questions, compare each respondent's distribution to the overall sample distribution.
    - AI respondents may show artificially 'balanced' responses (avoiding extremes, clustering around the midpoint) or may show suspiciously 'opinionated' responses (always selecting extremes).
    - Calculate Mahalanobis distance for each respondent's scale responses to identify multivariate outliers.

### Phase 4: Content Quality Analysis
- For each open-ended response, evaluate content quality signals:
  - **Specificity Score**: rate from 1 (completely generic) to 5 (highly specific with personal details). AI responses tend toward 1-2; genuine human responses tend toward 3-5.
    - Generic indicators: 'In my experience', 'Many people find', 'It is important to', 'The interface could be improved'
    - Specific indicators: 'Last week when I tried to...', 'My colleague Sarah noticed...', 'On the third time I used the feature...'
  - **Emotional Authenticity**: rate from 1 (emotionally flat/balanced) to 5 (genuinely emotive).
    - AI indicators: balanced 'pros and cons' structure, diplomatic language, measured tone throughout.
    - Human indicators: genuine frustration ('I HATE when...'), enthusiasm ('This is amazing!'), confusion ('I have no idea what...'), humor, sarcasm.
  - **Structural Patterns**: check for AI-typical structural elements:
    - Enumerated lists in open-ended responses (AI loves numbered lists)
    - 'On one hand... on the other hand' balanced constructions
    - Topic sentence + supporting details + conclusion paragraph structure
    - Excessive use of transitional phrases ('Furthermore', 'Additionally', 'Moreover', 'In conclusion')
  - **Coverage Breadth**: AI responses often try to be comprehensive, covering multiple aspects of the question. Genuine human responses tend to focus on what is most salient to them personally.
  - **Factual Consistency**: check for internal contradictions between responses to related questions. AI may contradict itself across questions because each response is generated independently.
- Calculate a composite content quality score (0-1) for each response.

### Phase 5: Composite Scoring and Classification
- Calculate the composite AI detection score for each response:
  - Score = 0.15 * behavioral_score + 0.30 * linguistic_score + 0.25 * pattern_score + 0.30 * content_score
  - Score ranges from 0 (very likely human) to 1 (very likely AI)
- Classify each response:
  - **High confidence AI** (score > 0.75): multiple strong signals. Recommend removal from dataset.
  - **Moderate suspicion** (score 0.50-0.75): some signals triggered. Recommend manual review.
  - **Low suspicion** (score 0.30-0.50): one or two weak signals. Keep in dataset with flag.
  - **Likely human** (score < 0.30): no significant signals. Keep in dataset.
- Document the specific signals that contributed to each flag (transparency for review).

### Phase 6: Manual Review Protocol (for Moderate Suspicion flags)
- For each moderate-suspicion response, perform manual review:
  - Read the full response set from this respondent.
  - Compare to the specific detection signals that were flagged.
  - Apply human judgment: does this 'read' like a genuine person or AI-generated text?
  - Decision: promote to high confidence (remove), demote to low suspicion (keep with flag), or maintain moderate (keep with flag and note).
- Document the manual review decision and rationale.

### Phase 7: Clean Dataset Production
- Remove all high-confidence AI responses.
- Apply manual review decisions for moderate-suspicion responses.
- Calculate the clean dataset: total responses - removed responses.
- Report the data attrition: how many responses were removed and why.
- Assess the impact of removal on sample representativeness: do the removed responses disproportionately affect any demographic segment?

### Phase 8: Aggregate Analysis and Reporting
- Report aggregate detection statistics:
  - Total responses analyzed
  - Flag rate by level (high/moderate/low/likely human)
  - Most common detection signals triggered
  - Semantic similarity cluster analysis summary
  - Clean dataset size and representativeness assessment
- Report per-question analysis:
  - Which questions generated the most flagged responses? (These questions may be poorly designed or easy for AI to answer.)
  - Which questions were most discriminating between human and AI responses? (These are effective for future AI detection.)
- Provide methodology notes: which detection methods were applied, thresholds used, limitations and caveats.

### Evidence Chain Requirements
- Every flag must specify which detection methods triggered and their individual scores.
- Every removal recommendation must be supported by multiple independent signals.
- The methodology notes must be transparent enough for replication.
- Chain: Signal (individual detection measure) -> Flag (composite classification) -> Decision (remove/review/keep) -> Impact (effect on dataset quality and representativeness).

### Quality Criteria for Output
- At least 3 independent detection layers must be applied.
- False positive rate must be estimated (if known human responses exist in the data) or bounded.
- Removal decisions must be based on composite scores, not single signals.
- The impact of removal on sample demographics must be assessed.
- Methodology must be documented transparently.
- Clean dataset size must be reported.

Produce all outputs in the specified JSON schema format.

metadata.author: ReClaw
references: Mitchell, E. et al. (2023). DetectGPT: Zero-Shot Machine-Generated Text Detection. ICML 2023.
Gehrmann, S., Strobelt, H. & Rush, A.M. (2019). GLTR: Statistical Detection and Visualization of Generated Text. ACL 2019 Demo.
Wu, J. et al. (2025). AI-Generated Survey Response Detection Methods. Survey Research Methods.
Yang, X. et al. (2024). Detecting AI-Generated Text in Survey Data. AAAI 2024.
Tian, E. & Cui, A. (2023). GPTZero: Towards Detection of AI-Generated Text Using Zero-Shot and Supervised Methods.
Jawahar, G., Abdul-Mageed, M. & Lakshmanan, L. (2020). Automatic Detection of Machine Generated Text. EMNLP.
Groves, R.M. et al. (2009). Survey Methodology. 2nd ed., Wiley.
Krosnick, J.A. (1991). Response Strategies for Coping with the Cognitive Demands of Attitude Measures in Surveys. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 5(3), 213-236.
Malhotra, N. (2008). Completion Time and Response Order Effects in Web Surveys. Public Opinion Quarterly, 72(5), 914-934.

changelog.changes: Comprehensive rewrite: 2000+ word prompts grounded in Mitchell et al. (2023) DetectGPT, Gehrmann et al. (2019) GLTR, Wu et al. (2025) and Yang et al. (2024) survey-specific detection, Tian & Cui (2023) perplexity/burstiness. Added four-layer detection pipeline (behavioral, linguistic, statistical, content), composite scoring formula with configurable weights, TTR/hapax/burstiness/formality feature extraction, semantic similarity clustering, cross-question coherence analysis, specificity and emotional authenticity scoring, attention check design, threshold calibration with ROC analysis, clean dataset production with representativeness assessment, per-question discrimination analysis, evidence chain requirements, expanded output schema.
